text: There is a revealing asymmetry in how knowledge workers describe lost time. Ask a manager what a canceled meeting cost her, and she will say 'an hour.' Ask a programmer whose three-hour coding block was interrupted by a fifteen-minute standup, and she will say 'the whole afternoon.' This asymmetry is not exaggeration — it is phenomenologically accurate. Neuroscience confirms that prefrontal re-engagement after interruption is energetically expensive and time-consuming. The manager measures cost in clock units; the maker measures cost in cognitive units, which have a completely different exchange rate. Organizations that conflate these two currencies will always systematically underestimate the price of interruptions.
question: Why does the passage say a maker's description of lost time is 'phenomenologically accurate' rather than an exaggeration?

options.text: Makers are more sensitive to stress and feel losses more intensely.
Prefrontal re-engagement after interruption is genuinely expensive and time-consuming.
Fifteen-minute meetings always run over into longer sessions.
Makers work in longer cycles so any interruption cuts proportionally more.
